title: A short proof of the multilinear Kakeya inequality

thm:1.summary: Multilinear Kakeya for nearly-axis-parallel lines: if $l_{j,a}\subset\mathbb{R}^n$ each make angle $\le(10n)^{-1}$ with the $x_j$-axis and $T_{j,a}$ is the indicator of the $1$-neighborhood of $l_{j,a}$, then $\int_{Q_S}\prod_{j=1}^n(\sum_{a=1}^{N_j}T_{j,a})^{1/(n-1)}\le C_\epsilon S^\epsilon\prod_j N_j^{1/(n-1)}$ for $S\ge1$. The Bennett–Carbery–Tao multilinear Kakeya inequality, with a short Loomis–Whitney-based proof.

thm:2.summary: Small-angle version: for every $\epsilon>0$ there is $\delta>0$ such that the same inequality $\int_{Q_S}\prod_j(\sum_a T_{j,a})^{1/(n-1)}\lesssim_\epsilon S^\epsilon\prod_j N_j^{1/(n-1)}$ holds whenever the $l_{j,a}$ make angle $\le\delta$ with the $x_j$-axes. The technically convenient form that the multiscale argument proves directly; thm:1 reduces to it.

thm:3.summary: Loomis–Whitney inequality: for measurable $f_j:\mathbb{R}^{n-1}\to\mathbb{R}$, $\int_{\mathbb{R}^n}\prod_{j=1}^n f_j(\pi_j(x))^{1/(n-1)}\le\prod_j\|f_j\|_{L^1(\mathbb{R}^{n-1})}^{1/(n-1)}$, where $\pi_j$ are the coordinate projections. The exactly-axis-parallel base case.

lem:4.summary: Multiscale rescaling step: for lines with angle $\le\delta$ from the $x_j$-axes, if $S\ge\delta^{-1}W$ then $\int_{Q_S}\prod_j f_{j,W}^{1/(n-1)}\le C_n\delta^n\int_{Q_S}\prod_j f_{j,\delta^{-1}W}^{1/(n-1)}$, where $f_{j,W}$ thickens tubes to width $W$. Iterating from width $1$ up to scale $S$ reduces to the axis-parallel case — the heart of the short proof.

cor:5.summary: Weighted form of thm:1: with $f_j=\sum_a w_{j,a}T_{j,a}$, $w_{j,a}\ge0$, $\int_{Q_S}\prod_j f_j^{1/(n-1)}\le C_\epsilon S^\epsilon\prod_j(\sum_a w_{j,a})^{1/(n-1)}$. The version with arbitrary tube multiplicities.

cor:6.summary:
Transverse-directions form: if the directions of $l_{j,a}$ lie in sets $S_j\subset S^{n-1}$ with $|v_1\wedge\cdots\wedge v_n|\ge
u$ for all $v_j\in S_j$, then $\int_{Q_S}\prod_j(\sum_a T_{j,a})^{1/(n-1)}\le C(\epsilon)\,\mathrm{Poly}(
u^{-1})\,S^\epsilon\prod_j N_j^{1/(n-1)}$. This $
u$-transverse statement is the form consumed by decoupling and Vinogradov-mean-value arguments.

thm:7.summary: Lipschitz-curve generalization: thm:2 still holds when the $l_{j,a}$ are replaced by Lipschitz curves $\gamma_{j,a}$ making angle $\le\delta$ with the $x_j$-axes. Same multiscale argument; needed for applications to curved objects.

edges.note: The $(10n)^{-1}$-angle inequality is reduced to the small-$\delta$ version by partitioning directions into narrow cones and rescaling each.
The multiscale induction bottoms out at the exactly-axis-parallel case, which is Loomis–Whitney.
Iterating the rescaling step from tube width $1$ to scale $S$ is the proof of thm:2.
The weighted inequality follows from the unweighted one by a standard pigeonholing/dyadic-decomposition of the weights.
Transverse directions are split into $\sim\nu$-narrow caps; on each cap a linear change of variables makes the tubes nearly axis-parallel, reducing to thm:1 with a $\mathrm{Poly}(\nu^{-1})$ loss.
The Lipschitz-curve case runs the same width-doubling multiscale argument, with curves replacing lines at each scale.
Still terminates at the Loomis–Whitney base case.

main_inequality.description: The multilinear Kakeya inequality in its nearly-axis-parallel, weighted, and transverse-direction forms.

loomis_whitney_and_multiscale.description: The exactly-axis-parallel base case (Loomis–Whitney) plus the width-doubling rescaling lemma; iterating the latter onto the former proves the small-angle case.

generalizations.description: Extension to Lipschitz curves making small angles with the axes.

BCT.title: On the multilinear restriction and Kakeya conjectures

Guth2010.title: The endpoint case of the Bennett-Carbery-Tao theorem

LoomisWhitney.title: An inequality related to the isoperimetric inequality
